messages.author: Comms_Engineer
messages.text: For the sake of discussion, a couple thoughts: 1) RNS's routing design seems to be inherently more capable of handling global-type networks than MeshCore. Because of this, being able to leverage existing transport such as MeshCore when alternatives aren't available is attractive. 2) Because of the amount of investment in MeshCore infrastructure already, I don't think it's reasonable to think that a buildout of RNS-specific LoRa nodes providing the same amount of coverage is in the cards. At a minimum, under current situations you're talking about every MeshCore repeater node adding a second LoRa module that's configured to run RNS-specific microcontroller firmware (doesn't currently exist), establishing a second standardized frequency, and adding a splitter/combiner to let both nodes use the same antenna. 3) I think the absolute ideal solution would be if MeshCore natively supported RNS traffic, with the current MeshCore implementation (public/private chats) acting as a sort of orderwire for the RNS network.
messages.content_html: For the sake of discussion, a couple thoughts: 1) RNS's routing design seems to be inherently more capable of handling global-type networks than MeshCore. Because of this, being able to leverage existing transport such as MeshCore when alternatives aren't available is attractive. 2) Because of the amount of investment in MeshCore infrastructure already, I don't think it's reasonable to think that a buildout of RNS-specific LoRa nodes providing the same amount of coverage is in the cards. At a minimum, under current situations you're talking about every MeshCore repeater node adding a second LoRa module that's configured to run RNS-specific microcontroller firmware (doesn't currently exist), establishing a second standardized frequency, and adding a splitter/combiner to let both nodes use the same antenna. 3) I think the absolute ideal solution would be if MeshCore natively supported RNS traffic, with the current MeshCore implementation (public/private chats) acting as a sort of orderwire for the RNS network.

1
1
messages.reply_html:
Symbioquine
It also seems like bridging at the application/chat protocol layer might make more sense in a lot of situations.

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: Re. #2, I'm less convinced of. We've seen a migration from Meshtastic to meshcore already, and I think some of the choices made in MC are going to be more-or-less insurmountable once the mesh gets big enough. At that point, I could see a migration happening. Re. #3, I think the best-case scenario there would be to use a payload version (co-opting packet version 4 or something) and just having the rest of the packet be RNS. I don't love either option. I think the likely best option is to have dual radios as much as possible at big repeater sites. One can run MC and the other RNS on different frequencies. If you're already investing in the solar, battery, etc in putting up a good site, another radio and antenna isn't that big of a deal

messages.author: Symbioquine
messages.text: There's even an argument for having both networks at scale from a "ecosystem diversity" perspective so there's redundancy in case of a vulnerability or other availability issue with one of the networks.
messages.content_html: There's even an argument for having both networks at scale from a "ecosystem diversity" perspective so there's redundancy in case of a vulnerability or other availability issue with one of the networks.

messages.author: meshyguy3287
messages.text: I run all 3... The retic network is stand alone. Frequencies I use are rnode at 914.875/125k/8/5 (copied the settings the big retic rnode meshes using east and west coast) I keep mc at 910.525/62.5k/7/5 and the mt node is at standard 906.875/250k/11/5. The rnode is just me range testing with android and home server so not much traffic at all, the mc is just me and a couple friends so also low traffic, the mt does get a lot of node info so some traffic is assumed.
